title: Toward Unified Standards for Open RTL-to-GDSII Design Flows: Insights From the Open RTL-to-GDSII Flow Standards Workshop (ORFSW 2025) [Standards Corner]
authors: Hossam O. Ahmed, Ahmed Madian, Boon Chong Ang, Yongfu Li
abstract: The rapid expansion of open-source Electronic Design Automation (EDA) has created unprecedented opportunities for accessible, transparent, and collaborative chip design. However, the growing diversity of tools, data formats, and methodologies has also highlighted the urgent need for unified standards that ensure interoperability, scalability, and reproducibility across the complete RTL-to-GDSII design flow. The Open RTL-to-GDSII Flow Standards Workshop (ORFSW 2025) brings together academic researchers, industry leaders, open-source developers, and standards experts to explore emerging challenges and opportunities in establishing a cohesive framework for open silicon development. This workshop provides a platform for discussing standardization gaps in RTL design, synthesis, floorplanning, placement, and routing, timing closure, physical verification, and sign-off. Through invited talks, panel discussions, and community-driven dialogue, ORFSW 2025 aims to accelerate the creation of practical, consensus-based standards that enable reliable design portability, enhance toolchain interoperability, and support the long-term sustainability of the open hardware ecosystem. By fostering cross-domain collaboration, the workshop seeks to strengthen the global movement toward democratized chip design and further advance the IEEE CASS Standards Activities Subdivision’s mission to shape the future of open-source EDA and semiconductor innovation.

title: Designing LLM-Powered Intelligent Interfaces and Optimizing User Experience
authors: Yizhen Huang, Weihao Qi, Yulin Tan
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 6th International Conference on Computer Science and Management Technology

title: DHR-LLM: A Dynamic Heterogeneous Redundancy Framework for Resilient Large Language Model Inference
authors: Ping Chen, Zhuyang Yu, Shuting Zhang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: We introduce Dynamic Heterogeneous Redundancy (DHR), an inference-time framework that improves the safety and security of large language model (LLM) systems by orchestrating a pool of heterogeneous models through dynamic trust adjustment. Unlike static single-model pipelines, DHR samples models according to confidence scores, aggregates predictions via majority voting, and updates each model’s confidence multiplicatively based on semantic agreement, thereby suppressing unreliable or compromised behaviors. We validate DHR on two representative tasks including SST-2 sentiment classification and BoolQ true/false question answering under both clean and adversarial settings. Even when partial models are compromised, DHR achieves strong resistance against attacks while maintaining competitive performance on clean inputs. Selection-frequency statistics show that attacked models are rapidly down-weighted and rarely sampled, demonstrating on-the-fly isolation without retraining or model internals. DHR is model-agnostic, and converts architectural/data diversity into a practical resilience mechanism for trustworthy LLM inference.

title: An LLM-Assisted Fuzz Testing Framework for Unmanned System Software
authors: Yupeng Zhang, Qing Wei, Shaoxun Liu, Yankun Xu, Haoyang Chen, Jinfeng Zhang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: The booming application of unmanned system has led the software vulnerabilities of these systems becoming increasingly prominent. Fuzz testing is an effective approach to detecting software vulnerabilities particularly during the software development phase. However, the requirement for manually written fuzz drivers renders it a resource-intensive and expert-dependent task, which hampers the widespread application of fuzzing. To address these issues, we proposed an automated fuzz testing framework based on large language models (LLMs). First, we employ an intelligent code parsing module to circumvent the context length limitations of LLMs, enabling efficient processing of target project code. Second, we engineer a structured prompt template to guide LLMs in generating high-quality fuzz driver code. Subsequently, to mitigate the inherent propensity of LLMs to produce flawed code, we integrate a feedback-based correction mechanism. Finally, we have deployed an automated fuzzing platform and applied it to several typical software components for unmanned systems. The system performs notably well on relatively simple fuzz testing targets and alleviates the manual workload required for fuzz testing complex targets. Ablation experiments confirm that the feedback-based correction mechanism effectively enhances the performance of LLMs in fuzz driver generation. This framework provides a viable approach to automated fuzz testing and is expected to contribute to vulnerability analysis in unmanned systems.

title: Adaptive Influence Regularization: Controlling Cross-Example Learning Dynamics for Robust LLM Fine-Tuning
authors: T. Aditya Sai Srinivas, Pooja Gautam, Gaddam Srujan, Sanjeev Kumar, M. Suresh, Madri Vijaya Raju
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: While traditional fine-tuning approaches are very successful in adapting the pre-trained knowledge contained within LLMs to novel domains, they are prone to instability because of the noisy nature of gradients and the tendency of LLMs to over-fit to outlier training examples, which results in poor generalization, calibration issues, and an increase in hallucinations. By quantifying example-specific influence and regularizing it via an influence-diagnostic framework proposed in Learning Dynamics of LLM Finetuning, AIR can both quantify and regulate the amount of example-specific influence on LLM fine-tuning, thus reducing the negative impacts of examples that produce harmfully high amounts of influence while preserving the positive impacts of examples that produce helpful amounts of influence. In this paper, we evaluate AIR under multiple paradigms of fine-tuning, including Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), and Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF), and using the Pythia and Qwen families of models, which range in size from 1 billion to 14 billion parameters. Our experiments show that AIR produces statistically significant reductions in the rate of hallucinations (by 17-23%) when compared to the baseline fine-tuning methods used in our study and increases the rate of preference wins in human-evaluated comparisons (by 48%). Additionally, we find that AIR tightens the confidence intervals associated with each prediction produced by the model and allows for the production equivalent levels of performance with a reduction in the number of optimization iterations (up to 30%) required to reach the same level of performance.

title: A Resilient LLM-driven Agent Framework for Multi-UAV Mission Planning and Control
authors: Haoyang Chen, Shaoxun Liu, Jinfeng Zhang, Yupeng Zhang, Shiyu Wang, Chenchen Liu, Peng Fu, Yuezhong Zhang, Dongchen Ji
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) offer a new paradigm for unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) mission planning and control. However, their inherent probabilistic nature and hallucination tendencies present significant challenges for safety- critical applications. Despite efforts to mitigate these issues through prompt engineering and post-generation validation, existing open-loop and code-oriented frameworks remain inherently unsafe. To address these limitations, we introduce Decomposer-Executor-Replanner (DER), a closed-loop agent framework designed to enhance the resilience and interpretability of LLM-driven UAV control. The Decomposer injects dynamic UAV states to transform high-level objectives into executable subtasks under explicit safety invariants. The Executor performs sandboxed tool invocations with runtime guards and feedback-aware parameter adjustments, ensuring bounded and interpretable execution. The Replanner monitors mission states, detects anomalies, and triggers recursive replanning based on contextual reasoning and mission memory. Together, these modules form a resilient and verifiable lifecycle for LLM-based UAV autonomy. Experiments conducted in Gazebo with PX4-ROS and up to 30 UAVs demonstrate that DER achieves superior safety, interpretability, and recovery capability compared to monolithic LLM and ablated variants. While current results are simulation-based, DER establishes a foundation for extending LLM-driven UAV control toward real-time deployments.

title: LLMRCA: Multilevel Root Cause Analysis for LLM Applications Using Multimodal Observability Data
authors: Gou Tan, Zilong He, Min Li, Haiyu Huang, Yilun Wang, Pengfei Chen, Giuliano Casale, Chuanfu Zhang
publish: ACM Transactions on Software Engineering and Methodology

title: SecureGov-Agent: A Governance-Centric Multi-Agent Framework for Privacy-Preserving and Attack-Resilient LLM Agents
authors: Jinyu Chen, Jixiao Yang, Ziyang Zeng, Zixiao Huang, Jinming Li, Yutong Wang
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 6th International Conference on Computer Science and Management Technology

title: From Structure to Semantics: Hypergraph-Based AR Assembly Guidance with LLM-Mediated Narration
authors: Xinda Liu, Bowei Zhang, Jiaju Xu, Jian Wu, Guohua Geng, Lili Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Effective Augmented Reality (AR) guidance for complex assembly faces a dual challenge: the inability of conventional liaison graphs to represent procedural logic, and the cognitive burden imposed by visual instructions. We argue that the solution requires a more expressive structure to overcome these representational deficits and a narration approach to mediate instruction complexity. Our method first employs an assembly hypergraph to capture the task's hierarchical information, from which an A* search algorithm generates an optimal assembly path. Then a Large Language Model (LLM)-mediated narration workflow is designed to address the ergonomic deficiencies of the machine-centric path. It employs an optimizer to improve fluency, followed by a narrator that crafts the steps into an intuitive instruction narration. A within-subjects user study (N = 24) revealed a progressive enhancement from our method's components. The transition from a liaison-graph baseline to the hypergraph alone improved objective outcomes by reducing task time and errors and improving subjective ratings (SUS, NASA-TLX, TAM, and ARI). Subsequently, augmenting the LLM-mediated narration maintained these gains while lowering cognitive load and elevating user experience and usability. Our findings indicate the value of our AR assembly design and discuss the opportunities of using LLM as a mediation layer for better user interaction.

title: RAG-Enhanced LLM and RL Scheduling: Optimizing a Multi-Agent Framework for Abnormal Futures Price Monitoring
authors: Shuangyang Zheng, Yangchen Zhong, Xiaobao Que, Jingming Shen, Baiyu Chen, Weiqin Shen, Shibao Zheng, Bo Liu, Jin Gao
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 6th International Conference on Computer Science and Management Technology

title: Adversarial Verification of the Machine Learning Models Security
authors: Maria E. Guseva, Maxim O. Kalinin
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: The critical deployment of machine learning (ML) models is vulnerable to adversarial attacks. The paper proposes a novel verification framework leveraging Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) to quantitatively assess ML model vulnerability. The presented method analyzes PSO convergence metrics, the proportion of successful adversarial particles, and perturbation norms to distinguish adversarial samples from clean samples. Experimental study has demonstrated that the proposed method effectively detects adversarial attacks by evaluating a model's local stability. This approach demonstrates the higher performance and effectiveness than the Satisfiability Modulo Theories (SMT) formal approach implemented in the state-of-art solutions, like Marabou. It contributes to more secure and reliable ML development, offering a practical method to mitigate risks associated with deploying vulnerable ML models in high-stakes industrial applications.

title: An Efficient Deep Learning Approach for Video Content Authenticity Verification
authors: Ashwini S, Nikitha K S, Savitha S K
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The increasing sophistication of Deepfake generation methods presents significant challenges for distinguishing authentic videos from synthetically manipulated content, raising critical concerns across social, security, and legal spheres. To address this, a robust deepfake detection framework is proposed that integrates frame-level extraction, Multi-task Cascaded Convolutional Neural Networks (MTCNN) for accurate facial localization, and data augmentation to enhance generalization and reduce overfitting. Leveraging transfer learning, the system employs a fine-tuned VGG16 Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for high-level spatial feature extraction. Trained and evaluated on the extensive Deepfake Detection Challenge (DFDC) dataset, the model achieves an accuracy of 97.24%, effectively distinguishing between genuine and forged content even under challenging conditions such as low resolution and compression artifacts. By processing videos frame-by-frame and applying a majority-voting mechanism for final classification, the framework demonstrates strong robustness and adaptability across diverse video qualities and durations. These results underscore the system's potential for real-world deployment in applications such as digital forensics, social media verification, and cybersecurity.

title: Comprehensive Security for Smart City Blockchain Infrastructures through Advanced Consortium- Based Transaction Verification
authors: Nikita M. Okhlopkov, Maxim O. Kalinin
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: The deployment of blockchain technology within smart city critical infrastructure, such as smart grids, intelligent transportation, and automated utility management, demands robust security and reliable data integrity. This paper presents a solution to key vulnerabilities in blockchain systems, including majority attacks and consensus manipulation, which threaten the stability of data-driven systems. There is introduced a consortium-based verification method that ensures transactional integrity through a dynamic reputation mechanism and threshold signature scheme, providing fault tolerance suitable for distributed control systems. Experimental study has confirmed that the proposed solution enhances security for critical IoT applications while maintaining the low-latency and low-power operational requirements essential for the blockchain-enabled smart city automation and control systems.

title: Battery Storage in More- and All-Electric Fishing Vessels: Real-Time Simulation and Experimental Verification
authors: Phillip Lewis, Milad Beikbabaei, Chandler Kemp, Mohammad Heidari Kapourchali, Ali Mehrizi-Sani
abstract: The transition of fuel-reliant fishing vessels to more- and all-electric alternatives is essential to reduce the fishing industry's dependence on fuel. Most research on the energy transition in the maritime industry has focused on larger vessels. Larger vessels typically have multiple generation units; however, fishing vessels have fewer onboard resources, making control and operation more challenging. This paper introduces the development of a real-time simulation testbed for small fishing vessels using two Typhoon HIL 604 real-time simulators. Data obtained from an actual purse seine fishing vessel are applied in developing, validating, and benchmarking models for vessel dynamics, propeller behavior, and hydraulic performance. The models are adaptable and represent a 58-foot fishing vessel, including its propulsion system, hydraulic load, and battery storage capable of operating in both grid-forming and grid-following modes. The testbed is then used to examine integration of a battery energy storage system (BESS): during transit, during fishing operations, and during both transit and fishing operations. The results demonstrate the distinct benefits of both small and large battery installations on fishing vessels, including shorter travel time between the harbor and fishing grounds, stable voltage and frequency across operating modes, and reduced noise during fishing operations.

title: Enhancing Result Verification of Convolutional Neural Network Predictions
authors: Yitong Sun, Lingling Wang, Yuxiang Wang, Xiaozhou Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: The widespread deployment of verifiable convolutional neural network (CNN) predictions has raised significant security concerns. Recent approaches focus on providing integrity verification, ensuring that each intermediate computation within the neural network is correctly executed. Although these techniques provide correctness guarantees, they typically incur substantial computational and communication overheads. These bottlenecks make them impractical in settings where the majority of provers are honest, or where constrained provers and verifiers require minimal overhead. To address these issues, we present a lightweight framework for enhancing Result Verification of Convolutional Neural Network predictions (rvCNN). Specifically, we implement verification for the output results of the convolution layer, ReLU activation layer, and average pooling layers, respectively. To this end, we employ Freivalds’ algorithm to verify convolutional outputs, and leverage the im2col algorithm combined with vector commitments to verify ReLU and pooling layers. Experimental results show that the proposed rvCNN is up to two orders of magnitude speedup compared to prior integrity verification approaches.

title: Reinforcement Learning-Based Critical Scenario Generation for Safety Verification of Autonomous Cyber-Physical Systems
authors: Hongbin Wang, Siqi Peng, Jiale An, Yuhan Hao, Xiaodong Zhang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Ensuring the security and reliability of cyber-physical systems (CPS) remains a significant challenge, primarily due to the difficulty of reproducing rare yet critical security-related scenarios in real-world testing. To address this issue, this paper proposes a Reinforcement Learning-based Critical Scenario Generation Framework (MCSG) for simulation-driven security verification of CPS. The method abstracts real-world driving data into a maneuver operation-level representation and models modal relationships among scenario parameters. By designing a hierarchical reinforcement learning agent with a composite reward function, it balances scenario realism, feasibility, and criticality. Experimental results in the CARLA simulation environment demonstrate that the generated scenarios effectively expose safety vulnerabilities in automated collision avoidance systems. Compared to existing optimization-based and learning-based methods, this framework reduces longitudinal and lateral trajectory errors by 32.7% and 15.3%, respectively, while increasing collision rates by 5.1%. These findings indicate that the proposed MCSG method efficiently identifies high-risk boundary cases, positioning it as a practical tool for CPS safety verification.

title: Non-uniform Air Gaps Regulation Method for Hotspot Suppression of Ferrite Cores in 11.1 kW Inductive Power Transfer Systems
authors: Xian Zhang, Yaxin Li, Yize Wei, Fei Xu, Zhixin Chen
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: The local overheating problem of ferrite cores in magnetic coupling structures (MCS) of Inductive Power Transfer (IPT) systems has become a critical challenge limiting high power applications. In this paper, a non-uniform air gap regulation method between ferrite blocks is proposed to reduce hotspot temperatures on the ferrite core. The impacts of four air gap structures (lg1 - lg4) on the hotspot temperature of the ferrite core, system coupling coefficient, and leakage flux characteristics are systematically investigated. Subsequently, a multi-objective optimization design using the Non-Dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) is conducted to determine an optimal combination of air gaps. To rigorously evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, a comprehensive performance index considering temperature, efficiency, and leakage flux is constructed. To verify the feasibility of the proposed structure, an 11.1 kW/85 kHz experimental test platform is established. Experimental results demonstrate that compared to a uniform air gap of 0.4 mm with equivalent coupling, the optimized structure reduces the hotspot temperature by 8.5% and the thermal gradient by 27.4%. Consequently, the comprehensive performance index of the MCS is enhanced by 32.1%, confirming the method's effectiveness in improving thermal reliability without compromising electrical performance.
